text: We present a case of a robotic trans-anal recto-urethral fistula repair. Our patient is a sixty-eight-year-old male who is morbidly obese at a hundred and thirty-five kilograms who had a Gleason eight prostate cancer treated with robotic prostatectomy without any prior radiotherapy. There was no immediate post-operative complication. Unfortunately, he represented three weeks after discharge with pneumaturia and pelvic sepsis requiring laparoscopic washout and a defunctioning loop colostomy.

A retrograde cystogram showed contrast extravasation from the urethra to the rectum, confirming a recto-urethral fistula. Three months later, after resolution of his pelvic sepsis, we perform a robotic transanal recto-urethral repair utilizing the DaVinci XI platform. The following is his CT retrograde cystogram confirming contrast extravasation from the vesicle-urethral junction into the rectum.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy with the da Vinci XI surgical cart on the left side. The boom extends over the abdomen, and three robotic arms are utilized: one for the camera and two for dissecting instruments. The gel point path platform is used with the robotic port configuration.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and monopolar scissors to dissect the fistula tract, confirming its anatomy by identifying the indwelling catheter within the vesico-urethral compartment. After circumferential dissection of the fistula tract, a superiorly based full thickness flap is developed, with careful dissection in the correct plane to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon develops a flap approximately two centimeters superiorly to ensure tension-free closure of the rectal opening of the fistula. The vesico-urethral defect is closed using a continuous 3-0 V-lock suture, with the bladder distended with water to verify the seal. The rectal defect is then closed with a continuous 3-0 V-lock suture.

The surgeon deploys T-cell fibrin sealant in the rectal vesicle space, followed by a CT retrograde cystogram confirming the resolution of the recto-urethral fistula. The patient's loop colostomy is then reversed.
